GTA 5 is one of those games that feels impossible to review normally because it’s not just a game anymore, it’s basically a whole era of gaming. This game came out in 2013 and somehow still feels more alive than half the games that come out today with “next-gen” slapped on the box.

The story is still fire. Michael, Franklin, and Trevor all represent different types of chaos. Michael is the washed-up rich guy who “made it” but is still miserable. Franklin is trying to level up and escape the same street cycle. Trevor is just pure unfiltered demon energy. That three-character system still works because each of them gives you a different lens on Los Santos, and Los Santos itself is probably the real main character.

The map is insane. You can be in the hood, downtown, the hills, the desert, the mountains, the beach, or some random gas station in the middle of nowhere and it all feels like it has a purpose. Rockstar cooked with the atmosphere. Driving around at night listening to the radio still hits different. It has that weird feeling where even if you’re doing nothing, you’re still entertained.

The gameplay is simple but addictive. Shooting, driving, flying, robbing stores, causing chaos, running from cops, doing missions, buying cars, customizing weapons — it’s all easy to get lost in. And honestly, that’s the magic of GTA. You can play it like a serious crime movie or you can play it like a complete idiot and both are valid.

GTA Online is a different beast. It’s fun, but it also became this wild grind machine where everything costs millions and half the lobby is flying around on weaponized alien bikes like it’s no longer GTA, it’s capitalism with missiles. Still, with friends, it’s hilarious. Alone, it can feel like a second job.

My only real criticism is that Rockstar milked this game like crazy. GTA 5 is legendary, but bro, we were stuck with it for over a decade. At some point it became less “wow this game is timeless” and more “please release GTA 6 before society collapses.”

Overall, GTA 5 is still one of the greatest open-world games ever made. It has story, freedom, chaos, vibes, replay value, and that rare feeling where the world actually feels alive. It’s not perfect, and GTA Online got ridiculous, but the base game is certified legendary.

Rating: 9.5/10

A legendary masterpiece that lived long enough to become a hyper-inflated casino.

It’s been over a decade, spanning three entire console generations, and Rockstar is still milking Grand Theft Auto V harder than a dairy farmer trying to pay off a mortgage. What started as a groundbreaking open-world experience has slowly morphed into an exhausting grind-fest. The single-player story remains iconic, but GTA Online is a relentless machine where everything from a basic sports car to a flying motorcycle costs tens of millions of in-game dollars. The economy is so aggressively inflated that it feels entirely designed to push you into buying Shark Cards just to exist in the same zip code as the fun stuff.

If the digital inflation doesn't break your spirit, the public lobbies absolutely will. Trying to progress your criminal empire or complete a heist as a legitimate player feels like a medieval punishment. You're constantly trying to coordinate with randoms who mysteriously disconnect halfway through a setup mission, all while navigating an endless plague of modders and cheaters. It is a daily occurrence to spawn in, immediately get blown up by an invisible hacker, and have a literal UFO dropped onto your character's head.

GTA V is a beautiful relic of gaming history that has massively overstayed its welcome. If you want to experience the phenomenal single-player campaign, it’s still worth picking up on sale. But if you’re hoping to jump into GTA Online today without a dedicated group of friends or a bottomless real-world bank account, you’re essentially just signing up to be target practice for a 12-year-old on an Oppressor Mk II.

The Ultimate Criminal Playground—Even Better with a Squad!
With over 480 hours clocked in, I can confidently say that the online mode is one of the most thrilling, chaotic, and limitless multiplayer experiences in gaming history.

Thrilling and Challenging Heists: Planning and executing massive heists like The Doomsday Heist or Cayo Perico with friends requires solid coordination. The rush of successfully escaping with millions of dollars in loot is unmatched.

Building a Business Empire Together: From managing Nightclubs and CEO businesses to Bunkers and Motorcycle Clubs, having friends to help sell and protect your cargo makes the grinding process highly enjoyable and way safer from griefers.

Pure Open-World Freedom and Chaos: When you want a break from missions, just cruising around Los Santos, showing off supercars, hitting crazy Stunt Races, or getting into random public lobby wars keeps the multiplayer experience endlessly entertaining.

If you have a group of friends to play with, this game offers hundreds of hours of pure, addictive fun. Highly recommended! 10/10.

One would assume that after more than 10 years of having GTA Online, the developers would address the slow connection issues to jobs, the inadequate job system, and other related problems when attempting to play with others. I have experienced numerous instances where I completed missions only to be abruptly disconnected, despite being in close proximity to my functioning router.

The most frustrating aspect is the time wasted and the minimal earnings generated. Aside from perhaps car theft and a few heists that do not encounter bugs 90% of the time, running a business daily feels like a futile endeavor. The entire system appears fundamentally flawed, and even the online events seem designed to be as unenjoyable as possible, suggesting that the developers harbor a disdain for the players.

While it is indeed GTA, one must be honest and recognize that the system is fundamentally exploitative, aimed at wasting your time so that you feel compelled to purchase their in-game currency. I am entirely convinced that they will replicate these issues in GTA 6, potentially exacerbating them to the point where players will need to invest 1000 hours just to afford a single vehicle.

[hr][/hr]

The decision to recommend or not wasn't that hard. Rockstar has decided that for me, actually.

I woudln't recommend the game even just based on the Rockstar launcher and always online policy - and that applies to singleplayer only as well. You don't have to be online per se, but the launcher wants to call home once every two or three days. Even ig you keep the game running and don't quit in the meantime.

[h1]Why this matters?[/h1]

Let's go into a scenario - you are about to fly on a long flight - say 7+ hours. You have your Steam Deck charged, you have your powerbank charged, you boarded the plane and after take-off, you whip out your Steam Deck, hit "Power" button to resume playing GTA 5 where you left off and you are greeted by Rockstar launcher that popped up and wants to phone home. This freezes the game, doesn't make connection and after restarting the game, you probably already know what comes - no internet, no game.

Ok, how many times you fly on long flights, I get it.

You are in camping site in the middle of nowhere, you want to just sit down and enjoy your free time with a good game. From now on, the same aw with the flight principle applies.

You are on vacation in foreign country and hotel wifi sucks (personal experience in more than one five star hotels). You won't play GTA 5. Or you may do a hotspot and download those 400MB luancher updates and based on your data plan the vacation can get a bit expensive. You will likely have to do this thing multiple times, as launcher phones home every few days.

I didn't even finish the game as I rage uninstalled it after the launcher wanted to validate my credentials by signing in AGAIN. Not only it ♥♥♥♥♥ up the game every so often, but after a few weeks, you have to input credentials again. No, I didn't input the credentials. And I never will. I will never finish GTA 5...

[h1]About the game itself[/h1]

...and I don't think I'm lacking by not finishing it. The game looks great, the mechanism of having three characters that have each their own personality yet so much in common is a great idea. The missions so far (did jewelry, submarine and bank-van-rammed-in-back-alley) were great. But.

As a main games GTA player since the first GTA game (didn't play GTA 4, actually), GTA 5 is somewhere around GTA3 for me. I will skip 1&2 which were their own style.

GTA3 was kinda good traversal into 3D, yet it lacked in the sense that player character was mute and only accepted mission in two sentences monologues - "That guy is bad because he something. I want you to do something with it."

Vice City was the one that got it right for me. Not only were dialogues introduced (even player character was speaking this time) but the game got the atmosphere right - the 80's vibe was ever so present in it! The story was also superb.

San Andreas is top GTA game for me. Not only it got the gang atmosphere right, but also introduced new mechanics (RPG-style skills) and had very long and very good storyline with many side missions that were fun. You also got the atmosphere of say hostile places in the city - if you got on Ballas turf, you are on enemy's land waiting to get shot at - you had to account for that.

I didn't play GTA 4.

And finally - GTA 5, the sole reason I'm writing all of this. While the game looks great and has some great things about it, it is completely sterile. It lacks in many things, mainly atmosphere of the city or even country. You can drive all the types of cars, go everywhere you want, buy many companies and get rich on investing. But the game doesn't really make you do so, it is only up to you. And you don't get anything out of it, youa re doing it for sake of doing it. This is why I didn't do it - I didn't feel like I would do it for something. So I stuck with the missions - which were great.

And if it wasn'ŧ for Rockstar launcher, I would have finished the game. It wouldn't change the conclusion of the review in any bit, mind you. The game is empty and it would have been even if I fifnihed it. I was bored of playing it when not doing a mission. Having many various activities doesn'ŧ mean they are fun to do.

I guess the game came too near to realism, even by being in today's world (not in past time as Vice City and San Andreas were) and stopped being fun for me.

I don't want to play real-life simulator, I want to live my real life instead.

And in my real life, I don't get stopped in the middle of walk or ride to sign in to be able to continue.

Rockstar made it to the same place as Ubisoft and Bethesda did - on my blacklist.

After spending 2244 hours in Grand Theft Auto V, it honestly feels less like a game and more like a second home. I still remember the chaos of late-night heists with friends, random car meets at Los Santos Customs, and those moments where we’d spend hours doing absolutely nothing but somehow never get bored. Cruising through Los Santos while the radio played in the background hit differently back then, especially during the golden days of GTA Online. The game had this perfect mix of freedom, chaos, and unforgettable memories that very few games have ever matched. Even with all its flaws and grinding, GTA V created moments that felt genuinely special and kept me coming back year after year. No matter how many new games release, loading into Los Santos still brings back the same nostalgic feeling I had the first time I played it.

This game is just too popular for a review in 2026, but I will try to give my best impressions as neutral as possible.

You start the game as Franklin, a black dude doing car reposition to a shady eastern european guy with one cool bro called Lamar. You will get 2 more playable characters as you progress on the plot: Michael, a 40s something dad who is in witness protection, and Trevor, a crazy guy who is played by Steven Ogg, the crazy Simon from The Walking Dead.

Expect the typical shenanigans of this apathic, full of crime world with a colourful cast. Missions are incredibly varied, and you will be amazed at all the movie like cutscenes, seeing characters giving you briefings or just fighting each other. The game has a main story path where you can just play the minimum missions, but there's a ton of content if you do side missions, collectibles and every little random event that pops up.

Talking about collectibles, story mode presents the ufo parts, the letter pieces and peyote plants as separate quests that will take hours to find every one. The rewards are not great, but they add a nice little distraction from the missions if you want to 100% everything. There's also the hated stunt jumps which require insane precision on unclear rules to get them done right. Add to that, another impopular quests of passing under every bridge in the game, and doing knife flights, which is flying your plane vertically between 2 buildings.

The story mode in particular, I found a ton of similarities with The Sopranos TV show. Michael story in particular reminds me a lot of the show, with the strip club, the psychiatrist and how his family life clashes with his criminal activities.

Visually, it might not look incredible nowadays, but it was at release. Most open world games abuse copy and paste or have too much empty space. GTA V doesn't feel like that for the most part. There's always something going on. Sound and voice acting is marvelous as well. It feels like a live world.

Is GTA V a good game ? It's amazing, a great experience. Do I recomend it ? Only for story mode. Avoid it at all costs for Online. The reason why, is that this Legacy version is just too bugged and is missing some content. Just get the Enhanced one.

For completionists, this game features one of the most infamous achievements ever made in the history of gaming, the dreaded Criminal Mastermind for Doomsday Heist. You are required to do it 3 times in all possible combinations (2, 3 and 4 players), without anyone dying in setups and finales, in order, and in hard. That's 29 missions where you will be screwed by the game multiple times. This Legacy version is unfortunately a tedious experience, glitches can end your run, lots of dead times going into Facilities and waiting for players, random crashes. It takes a level of skill and determination that I've never seen in any other game. This is about 8 hours of playing from start to finish, if anything goes wrong at any time, you have to restart from the beginning.

What do I want to see in GTA VI (let's see if it happens): the ability to enter more buildings, like a Sims game. Cars that use turning lights in a realistic way. Faster heist setups in Online, having a huge facility where you have your planning room in the other end makes no sense. And finally, instead of just adding Online content that no one plays, actually fixing bugs and glitches.

Even years after its initial release, GTA V remains the benchmark for open-world gaming. It is a sprawling, satirical, and adrenaline-fueled playground that offers endless hours of entertainment. Whether you are in it for the gripping narrative or the sheer chaos of Los Santos, this game delivers on every front.

Why I highly recommend it:

Three Protagonists, One Epic Story: The narrative structure of switching between Michael, Franklin, and Trevor is brilliant. It allows for a multi-layered story that keeps the pace fast and the perspectives diverse.

A Living, Breathing World: Los Santos is incredibly detailed. From the bustling city streets to the rugged wilderness, the world feels alive. You can lose yourself for hours just exploring or causing mayhem.

GTA Online: The online component is massive. With constant updates, heists, racing, and business management, it has evolved into a full-scale social and gaming platform that keeps millions of players coming back.

Sheer Variety: The gameplay variety is unmatched. You can pilot fighter jets, customize your cars, play golf, or pull off elaborate bank robberies. The freedom to do whatever you want is what makes this game timeless.

GTA V is a cultural phenomenon for a reason. It is polished, hilarious, and packed with content. If you haven't experienced the criminal underworld of Los Santos yet, you are missing out on one of the greatest games ever made.

Verdict: 10/10 – An absolute essential for every gamer's library.

GTA 5 is one of those games that feels impossible to review normally because it’s not just a game anymore, it’s basically a whole era of gaming. This game came out in 2013 and somehow still feels more alive than half the games that come out today with “next-gen” slapped on the box.

The story is still fire. Michael, Franklin, and Trevor all represent different types of chaos. Michael is the washed-up rich guy who “made it” but is still miserable. Franklin is trying to level up and escape the same street cycle. Trevor is just pure unfiltered demon energy. That three-character system still works because each of them gives you a different lens on Los Santos, and Los Santos itself is probably the real main character.

The map is insane. You can be in the hood, downtown, the hills, the desert, the mountains, the beach, or some random gas station in the middle of nowhere and it all feels like it has a purpose. Rockstar cooked with the atmosphere. Driving around at night listening to the radio still hits different. It has that weird feeling where even if you’re doing nothing, you’re still entertained.

The gameplay is simple but addictive. Shooting, driving, flying, robbing stores, causing chaos, running from cops, doing missions, buying cars, customizing weapons — it’s all easy to get lost in. And honestly, that’s the magic of GTA. You can play it like a serious crime movie or you can play it like a complete idiot and both are valid.

GTA Online is a different beast. It’s fun, but it also became this wild grind machine where everything costs millions and half the lobby is flying around on weaponized alien bikes like it’s no longer GTA, it’s capitalism with missiles. Still, with friends, it’s hilarious. Alone, it can feel like a second job.

My only real criticism is that Rockstar milked this game like crazy. GTA 5 is legendary, but bro, we were stuck with it for over a decade. At some point it became less “wow this game is timeless” and more “please release GTA 6 before society collapses.”

Overall, GTA 5 is still one of the greatest open-world games ever made. It has story, freedom, chaos, vibes, replay value, and that rare feeling where the world actually feels alive. It’s not perfect, and GTA Online got ridiculous, but the base game is certified legendary.

Rating: 9.5/10

A legendary masterpiece that lived long enough to become a hyper-inflated casino.

It’s been over a decade, spanning three entire console generations, and Rockstar is still milking Grand Theft Auto V harder than a dairy farmer trying to pay off a mortgage. What started as a groundbreaking open-world experience has slowly morphed into an exhausting grind-fest. The single-player story remains iconic, but GTA Online is a relentless machine where everything from a basic sports car to a flying motorcycle costs tens of millions of in-game dollars. The economy is so aggressively inflated that it feels entirely designed to push you into buying Shark Cards just to exist in the same zip code as the fun stuff.

If the digital inflation doesn't break your spirit, the public lobbies absolutely will. Trying to progress your criminal empire or complete a heist as a legitimate player feels like a medieval punishment. You're constantly trying to coordinate with randoms who mysteriously disconnect halfway through a setup mission, all while navigating an endless plague of modders and cheaters. It is a daily occurrence to spawn in, immediately get blown up by an invisible hacker, and have a literal UFO dropped onto your character's head.

GTA V is a beautiful relic of gaming history that has massively overstayed its welcome. If you want to experience the phenomenal single-player campaign, it’s still worth picking up on sale. But if you’re hoping to jump into GTA Online today without a dedicated group of friends or a bottomless real-world bank account, you’re essentially just signing up to be target practice for a 12-year-old on an Oppressor Mk II.

The Ultimate Criminal Playground—Even Better with a Squad!
With over 480 hours clocked in, I can confidently say that the online mode is one of the most thrilling, chaotic, and limitless multiplayer experiences in gaming history.

Thrilling and Challenging Heists: Planning and executing massive heists like The Doomsday Heist or Cayo Perico with friends requires solid coordination. The rush of successfully escaping with millions of dollars in loot is unmatched.

Building a Business Empire Together: From managing Nightclubs and CEO businesses to Bunkers and Motorcycle Clubs, having friends to help sell and protect your cargo makes the grinding process highly enjoyable and way safer from griefers.

Pure Open-World Freedom and Chaos: When you want a break from missions, just cruising around Los Santos, showing off supercars, hitting crazy Stunt Races, or getting into random public lobby wars keeps the multiplayer experience endlessly entertaining.

If you have a group of friends to play with, this game offers hundreds of hours of pure, addictive fun. Highly recommended! 10/10.

One would assume that after more than 10 years of having GTA Online, the developers would address the slow connection issues to jobs, the inadequate job system, and other related problems when attempting to play with others. I have experienced numerous instances where I completed missions only to be abruptly disconnected, despite being in close proximity to my functioning router.

The most frustrating aspect is the time wasted and the minimal earnings generated. Aside from perhaps car theft and a few heists that do not encounter bugs 90% of the time, running a business daily feels like a futile endeavor. The entire system appears fundamentally flawed, and even the online events seem designed to be as unenjoyable as possible, suggesting that the developers harbor a disdain for the players.

While it is indeed GTA, one must be honest and recognize that the system is fundamentally exploitative, aimed at wasting your time so that you feel compelled to purchase their in-game currency. I am entirely convinced that they will replicate these issues in GTA 6, potentially exacerbating them to the point where players will need to invest 1000 hours just to afford a single vehicle.

Downloaded GTA V for the acclaimed open world story experience.
What I got instead was:
a man named something unprintable who killed me on the loading screen
a $2,000,000 yacht I will never afford parked outside my $400 apartment
someone doing something with a rocket launcher that shouldn't be physically possible
and a CEO organization that collapsed because one guy drove a truck into the ocean for fun. That guy was me.
The economy is a realistic simulation of late capitalism. Everything costs money. Money takes forever. Someone who has played since 2013 flies past on a jetpack. He has everything. He will not share.
Online is a social experiment with explosions.
Story mode is a masterpiece I will never finish because Online exists.
10/10. I own nothing.
The yacht guy does though.

This game has a lot of positive reviews so i feel legally obligated to leave a negative one for different reasons:

I am a person who played Vice City, San Andreas and IV (all in original versions), but i still LOVE story mode of V, as amazing as the others but most of my hours are in Online and will be. I also like how many different ways to make money and grow in Online. 

But im here to leave a negative review for 2 reasons here:

[h2] 1. Online Shark Cards [/h2] So, for many of the people that are new or havent checked the Shark Cards for a very long time, the shark cards have not changed since THE GAME'S RELEASE. This means in 2013 you could buy every expensive property in the game, the most expensive being Adder supercar which was $1M, with a GTA$10M shark card (costs 100 dollars irl) but due to crazy inflation in the game now you can barely buy a few things at Warstock and Foreclosures and dont even have enough money left to buy a good vehicle.

[h2] 2. The Beef With The PC [/h2] As everyone is aware, R* generally doesnt like PC and its community, especially still going on with GTA VI launch. First off, THE LAUNCHER IS SOMETHING NO ONE WANTED AND NEEDED AT ALL. Really who would want your playtime being tracked with rockstarlauncher.exe or whatever its name is and launch first instead of the game itself. Second, they removed [b] text chat [/b] with the enhanced edition, showing how little they have actually done with the console to pc port. Even their fricking support page shows that way.

[hr][/hr]

TL;DR, Rockstar is awesome, top tier as a game developer but really isnt even remotely good at handling things like shark cards or PC platform.
